textPlain: There are many things I don't love about how tech has evolved, but when I look at a massive blob of XML and a lengthy explanation of "why this is better than just writing code!" I feel a little a bit more accepting of the current state of things.

Is XSLT enjoying the equivalent of Streisand Effect? Ever since news came out of Google wanting to rid Chrome of XSLT support, there has been a number of XSLT-related news here. I am not complaining, I think XSLT deserves a second life, it hasn't had the scrutiny it deserves, nor its "15 years of fame".

Sure, it's so much better to be writing massive blobs of YAML.There just seems to be some misguided human impulse to take every data serialization language and force it to be a programming language, instead of just using a programming language.
Agreed. I tried XSLT once. I made an XSLT for RSS. Coding in XSLT really sucked.

Current state of things is a mixed bag, in my opinion. XSLT wasn't perfect, and XML required a lot of repetition and neither got the amount of scrutiny both deserved to advance past their own inconvenience. But a lot of what we have today has just as many drawbacks, and that _despite_ WHATWG and Google hawking over it all. XML had namespaces, which alone were quite a potent feature that allowed to manage _semantics_ of data with more rigor and flexibility -- compared to the in my opinion amateur-ish "custom elements" HTML 5 approach, and how Apple refuses to implement parts of the spec., because they "don't believe in inheritance" (I am being liberal but those who know know). Then there is the HTML 5 parser itself -- "for the masses" -- with its idiotic and incomprehensible rules that are element specific, for which reason noone ever remembers these. Forgot a `

`? No problem, you can discover your omission after you've shipped! HTML 5 parser never aborts!
Yes, absolutely. I even made a comment to this effect… the loud minority here is mostly comprised of people who never used XSLT but want to argue against its removal on philosophical grounds.
